features.summary: Duke Realty reported strong Q1 2021 results with core FFO of $0.39 per share, representing 18% growth year-over-year. The company raised full-year guidance with core FFO expected to grow over 10% and AFFO growth of 8-12%. Industrial fundamentals remained robust with 100 million square feet of net absorption nationally and vacancy rates declining to 4.4%. Duke started $412 million in development projects during the quarter, 60% preleased with estimated 40% value creation, and raised development guidance to $950M-$1.1B for 2021.
features.management_outlook: Management expressed strong optimism about continued growth, citing multiple tailwinds including economic recovery, secular e-commerce trends, and inventory redundancy needs. They expect similar double-digit growth levels for the foreseeable future if economic recovery sustains, with potential corresponding dividend increases. The company anticipates strong development activity continuing into 2022 and beyond, supported by robust tenant demand across e-commerce, 3PLs, healthcare supply, and food & beverage sectors.
features.risks: Key risks include rising construction costs, particularly steel prices which have extended delivery times from 10-12 weeks to 24-26 weeks, though management has secured steel needs through year-end. Supply chain bottlenecks and material cost inflation could pressure development margins if rents don't keep pace. The company is monitoring oversupply concerns in certain submarkets, particularly Houston where they have no plans for new development starts.
features.key_drivers: Primary growth drivers include accelerating rent growth (11.4% cash, 26.2% GAAP in Q1), strong development pipeline with 68% in coastal Tier 1 markets, and strategic portfolio repositioning through $900M-$1.1B in planned dispositions including the entire St. Louis portfolio. The company benefits from 17% mark-to-market upside in existing leases, increasing Amazon exposure management, and secular trends driving inventory redundancy needs estimated at 0.5 billion square feet of additional demand.
